topic: Why I'm building in public — my 2026 learning journey
post:
Most people wait until they're "ready" to share what they're learning.

I used to be one of them.

After 4+ years managing social media across tech, e-commerce, education, and logistics — I realized I had been learning quietly for too long.

So in 2026, I'm changing that.

I'm currently building my skills in Digital Marketing with AI — WordPress, SEO, Shopify, Google Ads, Meta Ads. Not just consuming content, but actually applying it, making mistakes, and documenting the journey.

Why build in public?

Because accountability is the best teacher I've found.
Because someone out there is one step behind where I am today.
And honestly, because sharing forces me to actually understand what I'm learning — not just skim it.

I'm based in Ghaziabad, currently managing social media at Assert IT Solutions, and open to remote freelance or full-time opportunities where I can bring both strategy and execution to the table.

This is me showing my work — not polished highlights, but the real process.

If you're on a similar learning path in 2026, I'd love to connect.

What's the one skill you're committed to building this year? Drop it in the comments. 👇

#DigitalMarketing #BuildingInPublic #SocialMediaMarketing #LearningInPublic #PersonalBrand

topic: Why most business owners think SEO is dead — and why they're wrong
post:
"SEO is dead." — I've heard this from at least a dozen business owners this year alone.

Usually right after they've spent months on paid ads and realized they're bleeding money every time they pause a campaign.

Here's what's actually happening:

SEO didn't die. Their patience did.

Most business owners try SEO for 60-90 days, see no overnight results, and bail. Meanwhile, their competitors are quietly ranking on page one and pulling in free, consistent traffic — month after month.

I've worked across e-commerce, tech, education, and logistics. In every sector, the pattern is the same: the businesses that commit to SEO long-term end up spending less on ads, not more.

Paid ads are a tap. Turn off the budget, traffic stops.

SEO is a pipeline. Build it right once, and it keeps delivering.

That doesn't mean SEO is easy or that every agency does it honestly. Bad SEO is very real. But writing off the entire channel because of one bad experience — or zero patience — is an expensive mistake.

If your business is completely dependent on paid ads right now, it might be worth auditing your organic presence before your next campaign.

Happy to take a look if you're curious — drop a comment or DM me.

What's your experience been with SEO? Worth it or overhyped?

#SEO #DigitalMarketing #SearchMarketing #GrowthStrategy #DigitalMarketingManager

topic: The 3 SEO basics that move the needle for small businesses in 2026
post:
Most small businesses waste months on SEO tactics that don't move the needle.

After working across e-commerce, tech, and logistics — here are the 3 basics that actually make a difference in 2026:

**1. Fix your Google Business Profile first.**
Before touching your website, make sure your GBP is complete, updated, and has fresh reviews coming in. Local intent traffic converts faster than almost anything else.

**2. Stop targeting broad keywords.**
Small businesses can't out-rank big players on generic terms. Go specific. Long-tail, location-based, or problem-specific keywords win you qualified traffic — not just volume.

**3. Your page speed is silently killing your rankings.**
I've seen well-optimized pages underperform simply because they loaded in 5+ seconds on mobile. Run a Core Web Vitals check. Fix what's broken. It's not glamorous, but it works.

None of this is revolutionary.

But most small business owners are either skipping these steps or doing them halfway — and then wondering why SEO isn't working.

The fundamentals still win. Especially when your competitors are ignoring them.

---

If you're a small business owner or running an e-commerce store and want a quick SEO or Google Ads audit — drop me a DM. Happy to take a look.

#SEO #DigitalMarketing #SmallBusiness #GoogleAds #SearchMarketing

topic: How I approach SEO for a brand new website with zero domain authority
post:
Starting an SEO strategy for a brand new website is one of the most underestimated challenges in digital marketing.

No backlinks. No history. No trust. Google basically doesn't know you exist yet.

Here's exactly how I approach it:

**1. Fix the foundation first.**
Technical SEO before anything else — site speed, crawlability, mobile experience, clean URL structure. There's no point building content on a broken base.

**2. Go after low-competition keywords only.**
A new domain cannot compete for high-volume terms. I target long-tail, intent-driven keywords where the competition is genuinely winnable. Early rankings build momentum.

**3. Build topical authority, not just pages.**
I map out a content cluster — one strong pillar page supported by tightly related posts. Google rewards depth on a topic, not random content scattered across a site.

**4. Get backlinks the right way.**
Guest posts, business directories, and genuine outreach. Even 10-15 quality backlinks in the first 3 months move the needle more than 100 weak ones.

**5. Track, adjust, repeat.**
Google Search Console from day one. No guessing — just data.

Most brands want results in week two. SEO on a new site is a 6-12 month game. Set the right expectations and execute the plan.

I've built organic traffic from scratch across tech, e-commerce, and education brands. It works when done right.

Building a new website and not sure where to start with SEO? Drop a comment or DM me.

#SEO #DigitalMarketing #ContentStrategy #SearchEngineOptimization #DigitalMarketingManager
